text: The surgeon dissects the subcranial space, removing subcranial lymph nodes and exposing the left atrium and left main stem bronchus, with the esophagus overlying these structures. A Penrose drain is placed around the distal trachea. The left main stem bronchus is circumferentially dissected and transected from the trachea, followed by resection of a portion of the left main stem in preparation for anastomosis.

After resecting the left main stem, the surgeon places a stay suture on the distal portion. Excessive traction causes avulsion of the cartilaginous rings. The surgeon then clears more of the right main stem and proceeds to transect it.
The surgeon places a stay suture on the distal right main stem just below the carina, practicing suturing techniques in this area.

The surgeon performs the initial knot of the circumferential anastomosis using a barbed suture, threading the needle through the loop. The suture is tightened, ensuring it remains secure. The two ends of the anastomosis are then ligated together.
The surgeon inspects the circumferential anastomosis, observing a technical error with the knot not being tight. The trachea is elevated and highlighted by an arrow for visual reference.

The surgeon opens the pleura underneath the azygos vein and dissects the right tracheobronchial angle, with the vagus nerve highlighted by an arrow. The subcranial packet of lymph nodes is being dissected to clear the subcranial space. The surgeon then works on the left lateral side of the trachea, carefully dissecting the recurrent nerve off of the airway.

The surgeon performs circumferential dissection on the distal trachea, passing umbilical tape around the airway for traction. The crina and left main stem are elevated to dissect posterior to the veins and anterior to the airway. A stay suture is placed on the medial aspect of the left main stem and through the tumor in the distal trachea as the resection begins.
The surgeon completely divides the left main stem bronchus, followed by resection of additional margins from the anterior left main stem and distal trachea.

The surgeon resects the proximal and distal trachea, placing a stay suture on the left lateral aspect of the trachea and another on the left lateral aspect of the left main stem bronchus. The plan to implant the left main stem to the distal trachea is adjusted to implant it in the bronchus intermedius.

The surgeon performs a bronchotomy in the bronchus intermedius, resecting additional segments of cartilage to match the size of the left main stem bronchus. The procedure is conducted in a cartilaginous region to prevent collapse of the anastomosis. An end-to-side anastomosis is then performed between the left main stem and the bronchus intermedius.
The surgeon uses two running sutures of 3-0 PDS to secure the anastomosis. The second suture is locked, and the first suture is run to meet the second and then tied together.

The surgeon re-approximates the tracheotomy using a running 3-0 PDS suture, starting at the cephalad portion of the defect and continuing to the inferior aspect. Once the sutures meet, they are tied, completing the airway reconstruction. The stay sutures on the left main stem and bronchus intermedius are tied together to relieve tension from the anastomosis.

The surgeon inflates the lung to check for air leaks, confirming none are present. A bronchoscopic view reveals the tracheal anastomosis site, the neocarina between the left main stem and bronchus intermedius, and the right upper lobe takeoff.
